1.synthesis: This passage is parenthetical — Ellicott, Adam Clarke, and Calvin all note that 'the Lord had said to Moses' (rendering the verb in its natural pluperfect force) records a revelation given before the final interview with Pharaoh, explaining Moses's confidence in that scene. God promises: 'Yet one plague more I will bring upon Pharaoh and upon Egypt. Afterward he will let you go from here. When he lets you go, he will drive you away completely' (v.1). The people are to ask their Egyptian neighbors for silver and gold articles (v.2); God has given Israel favor in Egyptian eyes, and Moses himself is regarded as 'very great in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharaoh's servants and in the sight of the people' (v.3).
1.historical_context: The command to 'ask' (shaal) for silver and gold is not a request to borrow but to claim — a legal demand for back wages owed after generations of forced labor. John Wesley and Adam Clarke both correct the mistranslation 'borrow' in older versions; Calvin explains that God's sovereign right to distribute wealth justifies the transfer, as the spoils of a victor whose battle-right over ten campaigns was clear. The historical credibility of Egyptian compliance rests on two factors: God's supernatural inclination of Egyptian hearts (v.3, echoing Genesis 39:21), and the extraordinary status Moses had acquired through his long contest with Pharaoh — he was by now perceived as a figure of divine authority rivaling the Pharaoh himself. The silver and gold carried out would later fund the tabernacle's construction (Exodus 25:1–9).
1.literary_structure: The three-verse parenthesis provides the theological frame for the entire conclusion of the Exodus. Three elements align: (1) one final plague announced, with the promise that Pharaoh will thereafter 'drive them out completely' — not reluctantly release, but forcibly expel; (2) the financial compensation commanded, so Israel does not depart empty-handed after 430 years; (3) the honor God bestows on Moses before Egypt. These are not incidental details but the theological preparation for the Passover and Exodus: judgment, compensation, and vindication converge at the pivot point of Israelite redemption. Matthew Henry: 'One way or other, God will give redress to the injured, who in a humble silence commit their cause to him.'
1.theological_application: John Wesley on the silver and gold: 'Let every man ask (not borrow!) of his neighbour jewels — this was the last day of their servitude... the poor Israelites were so fond of liberty that they themselves would be satisfied with that, without pay; but he that executeth righteousness and judgment for the oppressed provided that the labourers should not lose their hire.' Matthew Henry: 'None be losers at last by their patient suffering any more than by their services — God will see to it.' Calvin on the divine disposition of wealth: 'as theft is the taking of what is another's, those things which it has pleased God to transfer to his own people must not be counted the property of others.' The Egyptian cooperation, produced by divine grace operating on natural hearts without their knowledge, shows God's sovereign governance of human willingness for redemptive purposes.
1.christology: Israel's spoiling of Egypt at the Exodus — carrying out silver and gold — became for the Fathers a figure of the church receiving the wisdom and wealth of pagan learning for the service of God. Augustine applied it to the 'plundering of Egypt' in theological education (On Christian Doctrine 2.40). Paul's language is more direct: 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). What Egypt used to oppress Israel — forced labor, withheld wages — God transfers to fund Israel's worship. What the powers used against Christ — death itself — God has turned into the instrument of resurrection glory. The redeemed go out enriched by the very kingdom that had enslaved them.

4.synthesis: Moses, still before Pharaoh at the close of the darkness interview, delivers the final judicial pronouncement: 'About midnight I will go out in the midst of Egypt, and every firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits on his throne, even to the firstborn of the slave girl who is behind the handmill, and all the firstborn of the livestock' (v.5). In every Egyptian house, there will be a death; 'there shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there has never been, nor ever will be again' (v.6). But 'not a dog shall growl against any of the people of Israel' (v.7). Pharaoh's own servants will bow before Moses and beg Israel to go. Moses leaves 'in great anger' (v.8). God reiterates that Pharaoh's refusal was foreknown, so the wonders might multiply; Moses and Aaron had performed all these signs before Pharaoh, but 'the Lord hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he did not let the people of Israel go' (v.10).
4.historical_context: The firstborn held singular legal and social status in both Egyptian and Israelite society: heir of property and succession, the embodiment of the family's future. Charles Ellicott notes that among Egypt's sacred animals, the firstborn bulls (including the Apis bull, the most revered deity in Egypt's pantheon) may have been specifically firstborns, making the destruction of the firstborn of beasts a religious calamity of the highest order. Adam Clarke on 'the maid-servant behind the mill': millstone grinding was the lowest form of slave labor; Dr. Shaw's description of two women grinding together with a portable millstone explains the social reference. Moses's departure 'in great anger' (v.8) is a striking characterization — Numbers 12:3 calls him 'the meekest of all the men of the earth,' yet John Wesley and Matthew Henry both note this holy anger as 'provoked by the hardness of Pharaoh's heart, as our Saviour afterwards' (Mark 3:5).
4.literary_structure: This speech closes the entire negotiation narrative (7:8–11:10), which concludes with the formal summary of verse 10. Three elements structure the final address: the death announced (vv.4–6), the distinction drawn (v.7: Egypt mourns, Israel untouched), and the prediction of Pharaoh's servants' reversal (v.8: they will bow before Moses and beg him to go). The midnight hour — the dead of night — is chosen for maximum dramatic contrast with what Goshen will experience. The dog-silence in Israel (v.7) is a proverbial expression (Ellicott compares Joshua 10:21): not even the slightest disturbance will touch Israel when six hundred thousand people march out at night. Verses 9–10 provide the theological retrospective on all ten plagues: Pharaoh's recalcitrance served God's purpose of multiplying his wonders.
4.theological_application: Matthew Henry: 'See how slow God is to wrath, and how willing to be met with in the way of his judgments — if the death of their cattle had humbled and reformed them, their children would have been spared; but if men will not improve the gradual advances of divine judgments, they must thank themselves if the worst is reserved for the last.' Gill: 'The firstborn of Pharaoh, who would have been heir of the kingdom, is placed in the first rank of victims; afterwards the whole body of humbler people is mentioned.' John Wesley on Moses's anger: 'He was provoked to a holy indignation, being grieved, as our Saviour afterwards, for the hardness of his heart.' Matthew Henry on verses 9–10: 'The scripture has foretold the incredulity of those who hear the gospel, that it might not be a surprise or stumbling-block to us — let us never think the worse of the gospel of Christ for the slights men generally put upon it.'
4.christology: The death of every firstborn in Egypt at midnight — averted only by the blood of the Passover lamb in the following chapter — is the typological center of the entire Exodus narrative. Paul states it plainly: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (1 Corinthians 5:7). The only-begotten Son of God undergoes the judgment that the firstborn of Egypt represents — death in the night, the cry that cannot be stilled — so that those sheltered under his blood are untouched. God's own firstborn-and-beloved (Exodus 4:22: 'Israel is my firstborn son') is released from slavery because the divine firstborn takes the death in Israel's place. The midnight cry of Egypt and the midnight silence of Goshen are the type of which Calvary's darkness and the Easter morning's light are the antitype.
